metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default value for `overlap_conf_threshold` in the Config section (e.g., 'default 0.7') so the LLMConfig implementation and eval are reproducible without a round-trip to the author.
findings.finding: The default value for `overlap_conf_threshold` is never stated in the plan; only `overlap_surface_cap` has a stated default (3). An executor implementing LLMConfig cannot set a sensible default without guessing, and the eval's precision/recall floors cannot be validated reproducibly until this value is pinned.
findings.location: Config section — 'overlap_conf_threshold', 'overlap_surface_cap' on LLMConfig
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'default 0.7' (or the intended value) next to `overlap_conf_threshold` in the Config section, matching the style already used for `overlap_surface_cap (default 3)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/config.py` to the Scope edit-target list and enumerate the two new LLMConfig fields (`overlap_conf_threshold`, `overlap_surface_cap`) and their from_env() resolution (env vars + _llm_float/_llm_int helpers).
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/config.py is not listed as a Scope edit target, yet the plan requires adding two new fields (overlap_conf_threshold, overlap_surface_cap) to the LLMConfig dataclass and their corresponding env-var resolution (REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*) in LLMConfig.from_env() — both are concrete mutations to that file.
findings.location: Scope section; Config section ('overlap_conf_threshold, overlap_surface_cap on LLMConfig')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'Edit `src/rebar/llm/config.py`: add `overlap_conf_threshold: float = 0.8` and `overlap_surface_cap: int = 3` to `LLMConfig`; add `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD`/`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP` resolution in `from_env()` via `_llm_float`/`_llm_int`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to Scope: implement and register in `eval_scorers.REGISTRY` two new deterministic scorers (e.g. `overlap_precision` and `overlap_recall`) that consume the `overlap_verdict` output shape, so `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` passes for `overlap_judge.eval.yaml` and `rebar eval overlap_judge` can enforce the stated precision ≥0.8 / recall ≥0.6 floors.
findings.finding: The eval spec mandates precision ≥0.8 AND recall ≥0.6 via `rebar eval overlap_judge`, but the existing eval framework's `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` rejects any deterministic scorer whose name is not in `eval_scorers.REGISTRY`. The `overlap_verdict` output schema (relation enum + shared_artifact + confidence + abstain) matches none of the existing scorer archetypes (review_result, completion_verdict, verification), so new pairwise-precision and pairwise-recall scorers must be implemented and registered in REGISTRY. The plan never names or scopes these scorers, meaning `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` will fail and `rebar eval overlap_judge` cannot run as specified.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Eval … precision floor (≥0.8) AND a recall floor (≥0.6) … Proving command: rebar eval overlap_judge'; Scope — 'overlap_verdict schema … register_contract'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'New deterministic scorers `overlap_precision` and `overlap_recall` registered in `eval_scorers.REGISTRY`, consuming `overlap_verdict` output (surfaced=True ↔ relation ∈ {duplicates,supersedes,depends_on}; gold labels from fixture `label` field); required for `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` to pass on `overlap_judge.eval.yaml`.' The fixture format doc (already planned) should specify the `label` field these scorers consume.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `eval_scorers.py` to the Scope edit targets (registering `precision_on_labeled_pairs` and `recall_on_labeled_pairs` deterministic scorers), or redesign the eval spec to use existing registered scorer names that together implement precision and recall floors.
findings.finding: The eval spec requires precision ≥ 0.8 AND recall ≥ 0.6 as gating floors, but the existing eval framework's `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` rejects any deterministic scorer name not registered in `eval_scorers.REGISTRY`. No `precision` or `recall` scorer exists in the registry (confirmed: only `emits_valid_findings`, `no_fire_on_good_cases`, `recall_on_seeded_defects`, etc. are registered). The plan names no new scorer registrations, so the eval spec as described will fail the CI discipline gate.
findings.location: Testing section — 'BOTH a precision floor (≥0.8) AND a recall floor (≥0.6)'; eval spec `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/overlap_judge.eval.yaml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add two new scorer implementations to eval_scorers.py (e.g. `precision_on_labeled_pairs` and `recall_on_labeled_pairs` operating on the labeled fixture format) and list `eval_scorers.py` as an edit target in Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the proving command in the Testing section from 'rebar eval overlap_judge' to 'rebar prompt eval overlap_judge', matching every other eval spec in the codebase.
findings.finding: The plan's proving command for the eval spec is written as 'rebar eval overlap_judge', but every existing eval spec in the codebase uses 'rebar prompt eval <id>' as the CLI invocation (18+ instances across eval_specs/ and docs). No 'rebar eval' subcommand exists in the CLI surface — only 'rebar prompt eval'. This is a cargo-cult deviation: the command format appears copied from an incorrect pattern rather than the established one.
findings.location: Testing section: 'Proving command: rebar eval overlap_judge'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Proving command: rebar eval overlap_judge' with 'Proving command: rebar prompt eval overlap_judge'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `rebar eval overlap_judge` with `rebar prompt eval overlap_judge` everywhere in the plan (Testing section and AC bullet 5) to match the verified CLI surface (`rebar prompt eval <id>`).
findings.finding: The plan's proving command `rebar eval overlap_judge` (Testing section and AC bullet 5) names a CLI subcommand that does not exist. The real surface — verified across all existing eval specs and `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py` — is `rebar prompt eval <id>`, not `rebar eval <id>`. A CI step or developer running `rebar eval overlap_judge` will get a command-not-found or 'unknown subcommand' error.
findings.location: Testing section (last sentence: `rebar eval overlap_judge`); Acceptance Criteria bullet 5 (`rebar eval overlap_judge`)
findings.suggested_fix: In both the Testing section and AC bullet 5, change `rebar eval overlap_judge` → `rebar prompt eval overlap_judge`. This matches the subcommand registered in `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py` and used in every existing eval spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Inline a minimal fixture format spec (field names and types, e.g., `{pair_id, item_a_digest, item_b_digest, ground_truth_relation}`) or add an explicit pointer to where the format is defined so the ≥12 labeled pairs can be created and loaded by `rebar eval overlap_judge` without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The Testing section states the eval fixture format is 'documented' but gives no location, format spec, or pointer to where that documentation lives. An executor creating the ≥12 labeled pairs at tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/ does not know the required file format (YAML? JSON? field names? label vocabulary?).
findings.location: Testing section — 'LABELED fixture (≥12 pairs at tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/, format documented)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the '4-way classification' label in the rubric-prompt scope: explicitly state which 5 → 4 collapsing occurs (e.g., 'the LLM chooses among {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct/unrelated} plus sets abstain:true when unsure') so the prompt author has a clear, unambiguous instruction.
findings.finding: The rubric-prompt scope says the prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification', but the `relation` enum has 5 values ({duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}) plus a separate `abstain` flag. An executing agent writing the prompt does not know whether '4-way' means (a) the 5 enum values minus abstain, (b) related_distinct+unrelated collapsed into one bucket, or (c) only the 3 surfaceable relations plus a catch-all. The prompt is a deliverable whose correctness is load-bearing for the eval.
findings.location: Scope section — rubric prompt body instruction: 'the 4-way classification'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '4-way classification' with: 'the 5-relation classification ({duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}) and the abstain flag (set abstain:true when evidence is insufficient)' — or collapse related_distinct and unrelated explicitly and call it 4-way.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile 'the 4-way classification' in Scope with the 5-value enum in What — either rename to '5-way' or explicitly document that 'unrelated' is a model-emittable class that the aggregation logic treats as a downgrade (equivalent to related_distinct for surfacing purposes).
findings.finding: The Scope section instructs that the rubric prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification', but the What section defines a 5-value closed enum (duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated). An implementer writing the prompt faces an unresolved question: is 'unrelated' a fifth class the prompt must teach the model, or is it collapsed into something else? This is a genuine cross-section contradiction between What (5 enum values) and Scope ('4-way').
findings.location: What section (enum definition) vs Scope section ('4-way classification' instruction)
findings.suggested_fix: Change Scope to read 'the 5-class classification (duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated)' and add a note in What clarifying that both related_distinct and unrelated are non-surfaceable downgrades, so only 3 outcomes ever surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Scope description from 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification' to 'MUST encode: the 5-way classification (duplicates | supersedes | depends_on | related_distinct | unrelated)' to match the closed enum defined in the What section.
findings.finding: The Scope section states the rubric prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification', but the What section defines a 5-value closed enum (`duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated`). The mismatch will cause the prompt author to encode four classes instead of five, omitting either `related_distinct` or `unrelated` from the LLM's output space — both of which are load-bearing: `related_distinct` is the safe downgrade target and `unrelated` is the non-surfaceable base case.
findings.location: Scope section — 'the body MUST encode: the 4-way classification'; What section — 'relation ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated} (CLOSED)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the 4-way classification' in the Scope sentence with 'the 5-way relation classification (duplicates | supersedes | depends_on | related_distinct | unrelated)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the relation enum cardinality: either change 'the 4-way classification' in Scope to 'the 5-way classification' or reduce the enum to 4 values and document how the fifth case is handled.
findings.finding: The Scope section instructs that the rubric prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification', but the What section defines a 5-value CLOSED enum (`relation ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}`). An executor writing the prompt body receives contradictory cardinality signals and cannot determine whether the LLM judge should be shown 4 or 5 relation values.
findings.location: Scope section — 'whose body MUST encode: the 4-way classification'; What section — 'relation ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated} (CLOSED)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change Scope's 'the 4-way classification' to 'the 5-way classification (duplicates | supersedes | depends_on | related_distinct | unrelated)' so the prompt author has a single, consistent instruction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `category: overlap` is a valid/intended value for the prompt front-matter, or use an existing palette value (e.g. `transform` or a new registered entry in `workflow-authoring-v2.md`).
findings.finding: The plan specifies `category: overlap` in the rubric prompt front-matter (`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md`), but `overlap` does not appear anywhere in the codebase as a recognized category value. The documented palette in docs/workflow-authoring-v2.md is `review/verifier/transform/code/exploration`, and every packaged reviewer in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` uses either `review` or `plan-review-criterion`. The `category` field is free-text (no enum enforcement in `prompts.py:228`), so this won't hard-error, but it is an unverifiable/novel value with no established semantics that could silently produce wrong behavior in any category-dispatch logic added later.
findings.location: Scope section — 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category: overlap)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit upper bound for K (e.g., `overlap_max_candidates` on `LLMConfig`) and document the resulting worst-case LLM call count and per-invocation cost estimate.
findings.finding: No upper bound on K (stage-1 candidate count) is defined, leaving the 2K LLM call fan-out potentially unbounded. A large K (e.g. 50) would produce 100 LLM calls per judge invocation with no stated cost ceiling or rate-limit handling.
findings.location: What — output schema / 'K candidates → 2K judge calls (both orderings), flat in corpus size.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_max_candidates: int = 10` to `LLMConfig` (alongside `overlap_conf_threshold`/`overlap_surface_cap`), cap the slice passed to the judge at that value, and note in the plan that worst-case cost = 2 × overlap_max_candidates LLM calls.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the intended `category` value for `overlap_judge.md`: either use a recognized category value or explicitly state that `judge.py` loads this prompt by direct file path (bypassing the catalog index) and acknowledge the `is_reviewer=False` consequence.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `category: overlap` in the `overlap_judge.md` front-matter, but `category: overlap` is not a registered or recognized value in the prompt system. The codebase derives `is_reviewer` exclusively from `category == 'review'` (prompts.py:143). Every existing prompt uses one of: `review`, `plan-review-criterion`, `plan-review-pass`, `code-review-pass`, `plan-review-pass`. The novel `overlap` category value will pass silently through `get_prompt()` (no closed-set enforcement on the category field) but `build_prompt_index()` will skip it (only `category == 'review'` prompts populate the index), and no documentation or plan prose acknowledges this behavior.
findings.location: Scope section — 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category: overlap)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC3) requiring that `register_contract("overlap_verdict", ...)` is called and verifiable, e.g. via a unit test asserting the contract is present in the registry after module import.
findings.finding: The `register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)` deliverable described in Scope has no acceptance criterion that verifies the contract is actually registered. AC3 checks the output shape fields but does not require a test or verification step for the contract registration call.
findings.location: Scope section (register_contract deliverable) vs Acceptance Criteria (AC3)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: 'overlap_verdict contract registered in contracts.py (test: assert "overlap_verdict" in rebar.contracts.registry or equivalent).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the Stage-2 bounded LLM judge's dual-ordering/position-bias rationale, aggregation rule, and the three-relation surfaceability decision before merging.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — bounded dual-ordering pairwise LLM classification with a position-bias aggregation rule as a precision gate — but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture it. Future maintainers encountering the judge.py + aggregation rule have no canonical rationale document.
findings.location: ## Why / ## Scope / ## What — output schema
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-stage2-overlap-judge.md` (or append to CLAUDE.md under a 'Overlap Judge Design' heading) covering: (1) why dual-ordering removes position bias, (2) the aggregation downgrade rule and its fail-safe semantics, (3) why only 3 of 6 `rebar link` relations are surfaceable, and reference it from the Scope section of this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add instrumentation requirements: emit a structured counter/metric (e.g., `overlap_judge.abstain_total` with a `reason` tag distinguishing `llm_error` vs `low_confidence` vs `disagreement`) so error-rate spikes are observable without log scraping.
findings.finding: LLM errors are logged but no metric or structured signal (counter, trace span, or alert) is emitted for abstain-due-to-error events. Operators cannot distinguish a healthy low-abstain run from one where most candidates are silently erroring out.
findings.location: What — output schema / 'Errors: LLM timeout/error/malformed output on a candidate → treated as abstain (dropped), logged'
findings.suggested_fix: In `judge.py`, after each abstain path, emit `metrics.increment('overlap_judge.abstain', tags={'reason': reason})` and include an AC or note in Testing that the error-abstain path is logged with a queryable structured field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/config.py` to the Scope edit targets and specify the exact `_llm_int` call signatures for `overlap_conf_threshold` (REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD, file key `overlap_conf_threshold`) and `overlap_surface_cap` (REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP, file key `overlap_surface_cap`, default 3) in the `from_env()` method.
findings.finding: The plan adds two new LLMConfig fields (overlap_conf_threshold, overlap_surface_cap) resolvable via REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_* env vars and [tool.rebar.llm] config-file keys, which requires editing _llm_int/_llm_str calls and the from_env() method in src/rebar/llm/config.py — but that file is not listed as an edit target in the Scope section. Config-surface proliferation without a named edit target means an implementor must discover this by inference, and the config keys will silently not resolve from the file layer.
findings.location: Config (LLMConfig, not VerifyConfig) section; Scope section (omits src/rebar/llm/config.py)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Config section, add: 'Edit src/rebar/llm/config.py: add `overlap_conf_threshold: float = 0.7` and `overlap_surface_cap: int = 3` fields to the LLMConfig dataclass, and wire them in from_env() via `_llm_int(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD", "overlap_conf_threshold", 3)` (adjust type to float via a new `_llm_float` helper or cast).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note in Scope that `overlap_judge.md` must satisfy `test_built_in_prompt_round_trips_canonically` (canonical front-matter form, no unknown keys), or place the prompt outside `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` if it is not a packaged catalog entry.
findings.finding: Placing `overlap_judge.md` in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` will cause it to be discovered by `_packaged_prompt_files()` (which iterates ALL `*.md` in that directory), and consequently parametrized into `test_built_in_prompt_round_trips_canonically` in `tests/unit/test_prompt_front_matter.py`. That test enforces that every discovered packaged prompt is in canonical front-matter form (`write(*split_raw(file)) == file`, with `warnings.simplefilter('error')` — unknown front-matter keys fail). The plan is silent about this CI side-effect and does not mention updating or satisfying this constraint for the new file.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New src/rebar/llm/overlap/judge.py + rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) ensure `overlap_judge.md` front-matter uses only keys in `FRONT_MATTER_KEYS` in canonical emit order and mention this constraint in the plan, or (b) place the prompt at `src/rebar/llm/overlap/overlap_judge.md` and load it via a direct path rather than the catalog scanner.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a schema-pin test for `overlap_verdict` to the Testing section (e.g., `test_overlap_verdict_schema_pin`) that asserts the Pydantic model's JSON schema matches `overlap_verdict.schema.json`, following the precedent of other contracts.
findings.finding: The plan requires an overlap_verdict.schema.json and a register_contract call, but does not mention a schema-pin test to keep the Pydantic model in lock-step with the JSON Schema file. Every other contract in the codebase has such a pin test (per the contracts.py docstring: 'a schema-pin test keeps each model in lock-step with its JSON Schema'), yet the Testing section lists no schema-pin test for overlap_verdict.
findings.location: Testing section; 'What — output schema' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the judge runs synchronously or asynchronously, and add a per-invocation timeout budget (or delegate timeout to a named LLMConfig key) so callers have a time-bounded done-definition.
findings.finding: No latency target or synchronous-vs-async execution mode is stated for the judge. If invoked synchronously on a user-facing path, 2K sequential or concurrent LLM calls have no time-bounded done-definition.
findings.location: What — output schema / Dependencies / Scope sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether overlap_judge.md should live in src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ (causing catalog/test conflicts) or in a dedicated src/rebar/llm/overlap/ directory, and document how judge.py loads the prompt (by path, not via load_catalog) to avoid the drift-gate test failure.
findings.finding: The plan places overlap_judge.md in src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ — the same directory scanned by load_catalog() / build_prompt_index(). The existing test test_every_reviewer_has_execution_mode_stamped() asserts that every prompt ID returned by load_catalog() has execution_mode == 'agentic'. Since overlap_judge.md uses execution_mode: single_turn and category: overlap (not 'review'), placing it in reviewers/ will cause this test to fail, or be silently excluded from the catalog only if the category filter specifically excludes 'overlap' — which is not documented.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Move overlap_judge.md to src/rebar/llm/overlap/overlap_judge.md and have judge.py load it directly via importlib.resources / a relative path, bypassing the reviewers/ catalog scan entirely. This eliminates the test collision and is consistent with how other non-reviewer prompts (e.g. workflow YAML steps) reference their prompt files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` (or the specific self-registration pattern in `judge.py`) as an explicit Scope edit target, clarifying where and how `register_contract('overlap_verdict', <builder>)` will be called so it is guaranteed to execute before any judge invocation.
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/contracts.py is not listed as a Scope edit target, yet the plan requires calling register_contract('overlap_verdict', <builder>) in that file (or in judge.py at module-import time), which is a concrete mutation to contracts.py or requires a self-registration pattern the plan doesn't specify.
findings.location: Scope section; 'What — output schema' section ('register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by reading `tests/unit/test_reviewer_prompt_hygiene.py`) whether it enforces a closed `category` allowlist, and if so add `'overlap'` to that allowlist as an explicit Scope edit, or document why `category: overlap` is safe.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `category: overlap` in the new overlap_judge.md prompt's front-matter, but 'overlap' is a new category value not present in the existing closed set of recognized categories (review, code-review-pass, plan-review-criterion, plan-review-pass). The plan does not enumerate whether existing prompt hygiene or index invariant tests will pass or fail with this new category, nor does it list those tests as needing review.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category: overlap)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope whether judge.py loads `overlap_judge.md` by direct file path or via the reviewer index machinery; if via the index, add the required `index.json` entry (with a recognized or newly-declared valid `category` value) to the Scope's deliverable list.
findings.finding: The plan places overlap_judge.md in src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ with `category: overlap` but does not specify how judge.py loads this prompt — via direct file path (bypassing index.json) or via the standard reviewer loading machinery (which requires an index.json entry and a recognized category value). All existing non-plan-review prompts in that directory appear in index.json with a recognized category; 'overlap' is a novel, unregistered category value with no validation boundary established. If the standard loading path is used, the prompt will fail to dispatch or produce a misleading fallback silently.
findings.location: Scope section — 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category: overlap)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'judge.py loads the prompt by direct file path (e.g. `importlib.resources` or `Path(__file__).parent / "../reviewers/overlap_judge.md"`) — no index.json entry required.' OR add an index.json entry for overlap_judge with an appropriate category (e.g. `"category": "overlap"` declared as a recognized value in a comment in index.json).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
